title: Remembering Alexander Morton: A Tribute to a Scottish Acting Legend
summary: Alexander Morton, a renowned Scottish actor, passed away at the age of 81. He is best remembered for his role as Golly Mackenzie, the loyal ghillie in the BBC Scotland series Monarch of the Glen, where he appeared in all 64 episodes from 2000 to 2005. Born in Glasgow, Morton trained at the Central School of Speech and Drama in London and began his career in the 1970s, building a diverse portfolio across stage and screen. Beyond Monarch of the Glen, he played notable roles such as the villain Andy Semple in Take The High Road for 14 years and appeared in films like Get Carter and Valhalla Rising. He co-founded the Raindog Theatre Company with Robert Carlyle and Caroline Paterson, contributing significantly to Scottish theater. Tributes from BBC Scotland's Louise Thornton and Carlyle highlight his authenticity, kindness, and impact on Scottish television and drama. Morton's legacy includes stage performances as historical figures like Macbeth and Robert Burns, cementing his status as a versatile and cherished actor in the UK entertainment industry.

background_read: Alexander Morton was a key figure in Scottish acting, with a career spanning from the 1970s to the 2000s. He trained professionally in London and worked extensively in both Scotland and England, appearing in TV series, films, and theater. His role in Monarch of the Glen made him a household name in Scotland, while his co-founding of Raindog Theatre Company showcased his commitment to the arts. Understanding his background helps appreciate the tributes from industry peers and his lasting influence on entertainment.
Article_Structure: This article reports on the death of actor Alexander Morton at 81, highlighting his career in Scottish TV and theater. Main points include his iconic role as Golly Mackenzie in Monarch of the Glen, his diverse film and stage work, and tributes from BBC Scotland and Robert Carlyle. The purpose is to inform and pay tribute, using evidence from his filmography and quotes from credible sources like Thornton and Carlyle. Author credibility is implied through BBC reporting, with methodology based on factual reporting and social media tributes, though it lacks deep investigative analysis.

perspectives.description: Morton's work in Monarch of the Glen and Scottish theater contributed to the representation and popularity of Scottish culture in media.
